title: Ensure end-to-end TLS encryption is enabled
description: run the following command t..."
automation_details:
The automated program will check the following:

1. **Enumeration of Function Apps:**
   - Use Azure CLI command to list all Function Apps within specified resource groups:
     ```bash
     az functionapp list --query "[].{name:name, resourceGroup:resourceGroup}"
     ```

2. **List Deployment Slots:**
   - For each Function App identified, list the deployment slots using:
     ```bash
     az functionapp deployment slot list --resource-group <resource-group-name> --name <function-app-name>
     ```

3. **Validation of TLS Encryption Setting:**
   - For each deployment slot, check the property `endToEndEncryptionEnabled`:
     - Example API/CLI command to check the property:
       ```bash
       az functionapp deployment slot show --resource-group <resource-group-name> --name <function-app-name> --slot <deployment-slot-name> --query "properties.endToEndEncryptionEnabled"
       ```

4. **Pass/Fail Criteria:**
   - **Pass:** The property `endToEndEncryptionEnabled` must be `true`.
   - **Fail:** If the property is `false` or not set.

This program will automate the compliance check for the end-to-end TLS encryption setting on Azure Function Apps and their deployment slots. The results of each evaluation can be logged and reported for compliance verification.

title: Ensure the 'Minimum TLS version' for storage accounts is set to 'Version 1.2'
description: In some cases, Azure Storage sets the minimum TLS version to be version 1.0 by default. TLS 1.0 is a legacy version and has known vulnerabilities. This minimum TLS version can be configured to be later protocols such as TLS 1.2.
automation_details:
- The automated program would check the Minimum TLS version for all storage accounts in a specified Azure subscription or resource group.
- It would enumerate storage accounts by using the Azure CLI command: `az storage account list`.
- For each storage account, it will query the property for the minimum TLS version using the command: 
  ```
  az storage account show --name <storage-account-name> --resource-group <resource-group-name> --query minimumTlsVersion --output tsv
  ```
- The configuration property to validate is `minimumTlsVersion`.
- The Pass criteria is when `minimumTlsVersion` is set to "TLS1_2".
- The Fail criteria is when `minimumTlsVersion` is anything other than "TLS1_2" (e.g., "TLS1_0" or not set).
- Example API endpoint to check if implemented via API could be the `GET https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/resourceGroups/{resourceGroupName}/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/{storageAccountName}?api-version=2021-04-01` for direct queries, returning the minimum TLS version property.

title: Ensure 'Encryption key source' is set to 'Customer Managed Key' for Azure NetApp Files accounts
description: Customer-managed keys (CMK) for Azure NetApp Files volume encryption enable organizations to use their own keys instead of platform-managed ones, providing full control over encryption.

- Utilize Azure CLI commands (`az netappfiles account list` and `az netappfiles account show`) to enumerate NetApp Files accounts within a specified resource group.
- Specifically validate the key source property in the encryption settings of each NetApp Files account:
  - Ensure that `keySource` is set to `Microsoft.KeyVault` indicating a customer-managed key has been specified.
- The pass/fail criteria would be:
  - **Pass:** If `keySource` is `Microsoft.KeyVault`
  - **Fail:** If `keySource` is anything other than `Microsoft.KeyVault`, or if the encryption setting is not configured.

```plaintext
GET https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/resourceGroups/{resourceGroupName}/providers/Microsoft.NetApp/netAppAccounts/{accountName}?api-version=2020-06-01
```

title: Ensure that a custom role is assigned permissions for administering resource locks

automation_details:
The automated program will check for the existence of a custom role with the specific permission to manage resource locks.
- Use Azure Resource Manager APIs or Azure CLI commands:
  - Utilization of the `Get-AzRoleDefinition` (Azure PowerShell) to list role definitions or the API endpoint `/subscriptions/{subscription-id}/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/roleDefinitions` to retrieve role definitions.
- Specific resources to enumerate:
  - Subscriptions and resource groups that are being evaluated.
- Exact configuration properties/settings to validate:
  - Check if there is a custom role named "Resource Lock Administrator" or an equivalent name.
  - Validate that the permissions include `Microsoft.Authorization/locks/*`.
- Clear Pass/Fail criteria:
  - Pass: Custom role exists, and permissions include `Microsoft.Authorization/locks/*`.
  - Fail: Custom role does not exist or the permission is absent.
- Example API endpoint: `GET https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscription-id}/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/roleDefinitions?api-version=2020-10-01` 
- Example CLI command: `az role definition list --name "Resource Lock Administrator"`
